content.text: An Availability Zone (AZ) is one or more discrete data centers within an AWS Region, each with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. AZs are the primary building block for high-availability architecture on AWS — by distributing workloads across multiple AZs within a region, you eliminate single points of failure at the data center level while keeping inter-AZ latency low enough for synchronous replication.

attrs.alt: AWS Region us-east-1 showing three Availability Zones (1a, 1b, 1c) each containing multiple data centers with independent power, networking, and fault isolation
attrs.title: AWS Availability Zones Structure

content.text: — located miles from other AZs in the same region, eliminating shared blast radius for fires, floods, and power grid failures.
— each AZ draws from different utility substations with independent UPS and diesel generators. A substation failure affects at most one AZ.
— AZs within a region are connected by private, low-latency fiber links (<2ms RTT). This makes synchronous database replication and stateful failover practical.
— a single AZ typically contains 1–8 data centers, each with thousands of servers. This means even an AZ is internally redundant.

AWS does not publish the exact number of data centers per AZ or their precise locations. The guarantees it does make: AZs within a region are at least 100km apart in most regions, and each AZ has independent power sources from different utility grid substations.

Most AWS regions have 3 AZs. Some mature regions have 4–6. Newer and smaller regions may launch with only 2. The minimum recommended deployment for high availability is 2 AZs; 3 is the standard for production workloads requiring quorum-based consensus (e.g., etcd, Zookeeper).

# List AZs available to your account in a given region
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[?State==`available`].{Name:ZoneName,ID:ZoneId,State:State}' \
  --output table

# Output:
# ------------------------------------
# |  DescribeAvailabilityZones       |
# +----------+-----------+-----------+
# | ID       | Name      | State     |
# +----------+-----------+-----------+
# | use1-az1 | us-east-1a| available |
# | use1-az2 | us-east-1b| available |
# | use1-az4 | us-east-1c| available |
# | use1-az5 | us-east-1d| available |
# | use1-az3 | us-east-1e| available |
# | use1-az6 | us-east-1f| available |
This is one of the most commonly misunderstood aspects of AZs. The letter suffix (
) is randomized per AWS account. AWS does this intentionally to distribute load — if every account put their primary workloads in
, that AZ would be overloaded. The randomization means your
may be a different physical data center cluster than your colleague's
Internal resource placement within a single account
Cross-account sharing via Resource Access Manager

import boto3

ec2 = boto3.client("ec2", region_name="us-east-1")
response = ec2.describe_availability_zones()

print("AZ Name → AZ ID mapping for this account:")
for az in response["AvailabilityZones"]:
    print(f"  {az['ZoneName']:<18} → {az['ZoneId']}")

# Output (varies by account due to randomization):
# us-east-1a         → use1-az1
# us-east-1b         → use1-az2
# us-east-1c         → use1-az4
# us-east-1d         → use1-az5
# us-east-1e         → use1-az3
# us-east-1f         → use1-az6

# Another account's mapping might be:
# us-east-1a         → use1-az4   ← different physical AZ than above
# us-east-1b         → use1-az1
# us-east-1c         → use1-az2

Each VPC subnet is associated with exactly one Availability Zone. This is the fundamental mechanism for placing resources in specific AZs — you create one subnet per AZ, then deploy EC2 instances, RDS read replicas, Lambda ENIs, and other resources into the appropriate subnet.

# Create a VPC with subnets spread across 3 AZs
VPC_ID=$(aws ec2 create-vpc \
  --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --query 'Vpc.VpcId' --output text)

# One subnet per AZ — AZ specified directly
SUBNET_1A=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
  --cidr-block 10.0.1.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

SUBNET_1B=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
  --cidr-block 10.0.2.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1b \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

SUBNET_1C=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
  --cidr-block 10.0.3.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1c \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

echo "Created subnets: $SUBNET_1A $SUBNET_1B $SUBNET_1C"

Best practice: create both public and private subnets in each AZ. Typical layout: public subnets (10.0.1-3.0/24) for load balancers, private subnets (10.0.11-13.0/24) for application servers, and data subnets (10.0.21-23.0/24) for databases — all replicated across 3 AZs.
Instance is placed in the AZ of the subnet you choose
Auto Scaling Group with subnets in 3 AZs; min 1 instance per AZ
Primary in one AZ; Multi-AZ standby in another AZ; synchronous replication
Enable Multi-AZ; failover is automatic (<60s in most cases)
Nodes deployed in each AZ you enable; traffic routed to healthy targets
Enable AZs for all AZs that contain targets; cross-zone load balancing optional
Volume exists in a single AZ; cannot be attached to instance in different AZ
Use EBS snapshots to copy across AZs; or use EFS (multi-AZ by default)
Cluster nodes placed in subnet AZs you specify
Multi-AZ with auto-failover for Redis; spread nodes across 3 AZs
AWS places functions in multiple AZs automatically (managed)
No config needed; for VPC Lambda, ensure subnets span ≥2 AZs
Data replicated across ≥3 AZs within a region automatically
No configuration needed; S3 is inherently multi-AZ
Inter-AZ Latency and Data Transfer Costs

AZs within a region are connected by AWS's dedicated private fiber backbone. Typical round-trip latency between AZs is under 2ms — fast enough for synchronous database replication and cluster heartbeats. However, data transfer between AZs within the same region is not free:
Both source and destination must use private IPs
$0.02/GB total for bidirectional; can add up for data-intensive services
Avoid — use private IPs and VPC routing for all inter-AZ traffic
ALB: enabled free by default; NLB/GWLB: charged when enabled

For data-intensive applications (large database replicas, Kafka brokers, Elasticsearch clusters), inter-AZ data transfer costs can become a significant line item. A 10TB/day cross-AZ replication stream costs ~$100/day ($3,000/month). Architect to minimize unnecessary cross-AZ traffic while preserving the HA guarantees.
Controlling AZ Placement: Placement Groups and Tenancy
Placement groups let you control how EC2 instances are distributed across hardware within and across AZs:
All instances in a single AZ, same rack
HPC / ML training needing lowest-latency instance-to-instance (10Gbps+)
Instances on distinct hardware racks, can span AZs
Small critical clusters (≤7 instances per AZ); maximum fault isolation
Groups of instances in separate partitions; each partition = distinct rack set
Large distributed systems (Hadoop, Cassandra, Kafka) where rack awareness matters

# Create a spread placement group across AZs (max fault isolation)
aws ec2 create-placement-group \
  --group-name critical-db-spread \
  --strategy spread

# Launch instances into the spread group in specific AZs
for AZ in us-east-1a us-east-1b us-east-1c; do
  aws ec2 run-instances \
    --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
    --instance-type r5.2xlarge \
    --placement "AvailabilityZone=${AZ},GroupName=critical-db-spread" \
    --subnet-id $(aws ec2 describe-subnets \
      --filters "Name=availabilityZone,Values=${AZ}" "Name=tag:Name,Values=private-*" \
      --query 'Subnets[0].SubnetId' --output text) \
    --count 1
done
AZ failures are rare but real — AWS has had AZ-level impairments in us-east-1, us-east-2, and eu-west-1 over the years. The pattern for surviving an AZ failure is:
— with 3 AZs, losing one leaves you at 66% capacity. With 2 AZs, losing one halves capacity.
— if you need 100 servers at peak, run 150 spread across 3 AZs (50 each). Losing one AZ leaves 100 servers — exactly peak capacity.
Use Auto Scaling with AZ rebalancing
— ASG automatically launches replacement instances in surviving AZs when one becomes impaired.
— RDS Multi-AZ, ElastiCache Multi-AZ, and EFS (inherently multi-AZ) handle automatic failover.

# Auto Scaling Group spanning 3 AZs with AZ rebalancing
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \
  --launch-template LaunchTemplateId=lt-0abcdef1234567890,Version=1 \
  --min-size 6 \
  --max-size 18 \
  --desired-capacity 9 \
  --vpc-zone-identifier "$SUBNET_1A,$SUBNET_1B,$SUBNET_1C" \
  --health-check-type ELB \
  --health-check-grace-period 300

# Verify AZ distribution of running instances
aws autoscaling describe-auto-scaling-groups \
  --auto-scaling-group-names web-asg \
  --query 'AutoScalingGroups[0].Instances[*].{AZ:AvailabilityZone,State:HealthStatus}' \
  --output table

hold compute capacity in a specific AZ so it is always available when you need it — regardless of spot demand or regional capacity events. They are billed at the On-Demand rate whether or not instances run against them.
Any instance in the AZ matching type/OS
Only your account / specific launch template
Predictable batch jobs or guaranteed launch

# Create capacity reservation in us-east-1a for 10 × m5.xlarge Linux
CR_ID=$(aws ec2 create-capacity-reservation \
  --instance-type m5.xlarge \
  --instance-platform Linux/UNIX \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --instance-count 10 \
  --instance-match-criteria open \
  --query 'CapacityReservation.CapacityReservationId' \
  --output text)
echo "Reserved: $CR_ID"

# List available capacity across AZs for a given instance type
aws ec2 describe-instance-type-offerings \
  --location-type availability-zone \
  --filters Name=instance-type,Values=m5.xlarge \
  --query 'InstanceTypeOfferings[*].{AZ:Location}' \
  --output table
EC2 Nitro System and AZ-Level Isolation
AWS runs all modern EC2 instances on the

— a lightweight, purpose-built stack that offloads network and storage I/O to dedicated Nitro cards. Each AZ runs its own pool of Nitro hosts. Failures within one AZ (power, networking, hardware) cannot propagate to another because:
: each AZ is served by one or more independent UPS and generator systems.
: distinct CRAC units prevent thermal runaway propagation.
: VPC subnets are AZ-scoped; inter-AZ traffic traverses the redundant fiber backbone, not the intra-AZ fabric.
: the hypervisor has no SSH or operator access — reducing the blast radius of software vulnerabilities per AZ.

# Confirm your instance is running on Nitro (no hypervisor field = bare-metal or Nitro)
INSTANCE_ID=$(curl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/instance-id)
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids "$INSTANCE_ID" \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].{Hypervisor:Hypervisor,Platform:PlatformDetails,AZ:Placement.AvailabilityZone}'

# List Nitro-based instance types (hypervisor=nitro)
aws ec2 describe-instance-types \
  --filters Name=hypervisor,Values=nitro \
  --query 'InstanceTypes[*].InstanceType' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | sort | head -20
Amazon EBS volumes are locked to the AZ in which they are created. An instance in
cannot attach a volume that lives in
. This AZ affinity is intentional — block storage I/O must traverse the lowest-latency path within the same AZ fabric.
Up to 16 Nitro instances, same AZ

# Move an EBS volume to a different AZ via snapshot
VOL_ID="vol-0abcdef1234567890"
TARGET_AZ="us-east-1c"

# Step 1: snapshot
SNAP_ID=$(aws ec2 create-snapshot \
  --volume-id "$VOL_ID" \
  --description "AZ migration snapshot" \
  --query 'SnapshotId' --output text)

# Step 2: wait for snapshot completion
aws ec2 wait snapshot-completed --snapshot-ids "$SNAP_ID"

# Step 3: create new volume in target AZ
NEW_VOL=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --snapshot-id "$SNAP_ID" \
  --availability-zone "$TARGET_AZ" \
  --volume-type gp3 \
  --query 'VolumeId' --output text)
echo "New volume in $TARGET_AZ: $NEW_VOL"
Managed database services expose AZ placement as a first-class concern. Understanding how each service uses AZs lets you design for durability without manual replication logic.
Synchronous standby in a different AZ
Async — seconds of data loss possible

# Create an RDS Multi-AZ MySQL instance with explicit primary AZ
aws rds create-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-mysql \
  --db-instance-class db.m5.large \
  --engine mysql \
  --engine-version 8.0 \
  --master-username admin \
  --master-user-password 'S3cur3Pass!123' \
  --allocated-storage 100 \
  --storage-type gp3 \
  --multi-az \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --backup-retention-period 7

# Trigger a manual failover to test (promotion of standby)
aws rds reboot-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-mysql \
  --force-failover
Route 53 Health Checks and AZ-Aware Routing

Route 53 health checks operate from multiple AWS edge locations (not AZs) and can be combined with latency-based or failover routing to route traffic away from an unhealthy AZ automatically. When an AZ-scoped endpoint (ALB in a single AZ, an EC2 Elastic IP) becomes unhealthy, Route 53 removes it from DNS responses within the TTL window.

# Create a health check for an AZ-specific endpoint
HC_ID=$(aws route53 create-health-check \
  --caller-reference "az-hc-$(date +%s)" \
  --health-check-config '{
    "IPAddress": "203.0.113.10",
    "Port": 443,
    "Type": "HTTPS",
    "ResourcePath": "/health",
    "FailureThreshold": 3,
    "RequestInterval": 10
  }' \
  --query 'HealthCheck.Id' --output text)

# Tag the health check by AZ for clarity
aws route53 change-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-type healthcheck \
  --resource-id "$HC_ID" \
  --add-tags Key=AZ,Value=us-east-1a Key=Env,Value=prod

# Associate with a weighted record (weight 0 = disabled, 1 = active)
cat > /tmp/dns-change.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Changes": [{
    "Action": "UPSERT",
    "ResourceRecordSet": {
      "Name": "api.example.com",
      "Type": "A",
      "SetIdentifier": "az-1a",
      "Weight": 33,
      "TTL": 30,
      "ResourceRecords": [{"Value": "203.0.113.10"}],
      "HealthCheckId": "HC_ID_PLACEHOLDER"
    }
  }]
}
EOF
echo "Health check $HC_ID ready for DNS association"
Every byte that crosses an AZ boundary incurs a data-transfer charge (typically
for EC2/RDS/ECS). At high throughput this becomes a significant line item. The following patterns keep cross-AZ traffic to a minimum.
Disabled by default for ALB (check per-LB attribute)
ECS Service Connect AZ-aware routing, EKS topology hints
Read replicas in same AZ as reader
Specify AZ when creating replica; use reader endpoint
Free — S3 data transfer in-region is $0
Read from replica in same AZ as client

# Check ALB cross-zone load balancing setting
aws elbv2 describe-load-balancer-attributes \
  --load-balancer-arn "$ALB_ARN" \
  --query 'Attributes[?Key==`load_balancing.cross_zone.enabled`]'

# Disable cross-zone LB to reduce cross-AZ traffic costs
aws elbv2 modify-load-balancer-attributes \
  --load-balancer-arn "$ALB_ARN" \
  --attributes Key=load_balancing.cross_zone.enabled,Value=false

# Monitor cross-AZ data transfer costs via Cost Explorer (granular by AZ)
aws ce get-cost-and-usage \
  --time-period Start=2024-01-01,End=2024-01-31 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --filter '{"Dimensions":{"Key":"USAGE_TYPE_GROUP","Values":["EC2: Data Transfer - Inter AZ"]}}' \
  --metrics BlendedCost

When AWS declares an AZ impairment (via Service Health Dashboard or a CloudWatch alarm spike on a single AZ), the recommended response follows a triage order that minimizes blast radius while keeping the application running in remaining AZs.
: Remove the impaired AZ's subnet from the ALB target group's AZ affinity list (or disable cross-zone LB so the impaired AZ receives zero traffic).
: Increase ASG desired capacity so the remaining AZs absorb the load. Check that your capacity reservation or Savings Plan covers the burst.
: RDS Multi-AZ promotes automatically. Aurora reroutes via the cluster endpoint. Confirm read-replica clients are not still pointing at the impaired AZ endpoint.
: EBS volumes in the impaired AZ cannot be attached elsewhere. Use the latest snapshot (or an AWS Backup recovery point) to restore in a healthy AZ.
: Once the AZ recovers, re-enable it gradually (weighted DNS or capacity group weight) rather than instantly to avoid thundering-herd on the newly available AZ.

# CloudWatch composite alarm for AZ impairment (fire when ALL three conditions hold)
aws cloudwatch put-composite-alarm \
  --alarm-name az-1a-impaired \
  --alarm-rule 'ALARM("ec2-status-1a") AND ALARM("alb-unhealthy-1a") AND ALARM("rds-event-1a")' \
  --actions-enabled \
  --alarm-actions "arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:ops-pager"

# Drain the impaired AZ from an ALB (set AZ to disabled)
aws elbv2 set-subnets \
  --load-balancer-arn "$ALB_ARN" \
  --subnets "$SUBNET_1B" "$SUBNET_1C"   # omit 1a subnet to drain it

# Increase ASG capacity to compensate for lost AZ
aws autoscaling set-desired-capacity \
  --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \
  --desired-capacity 12   # was 9 across 3 AZs; now 12 across 2
An AZ is one or more data centers with independent power, networking, and cooling — physically separated from other AZs in the same region.
) are stable and should be used for cross-account resource sharing via RAM.
Each subnet is pinned to a single AZ; controlling which subnet you deploy into controls which AZ your resource lands in.
Inter-AZ traffic within a region costs $0.01/GB per direction — design to minimize cross-AZ data movement while preserving HA.
For AZ-failure resilience: deploy in ≥3 AZs, provision 150% of peak capacity, and enable Multi-AZ for every stateful managed service.
